title: Z-residual Diagnostics for brms Logistic Regression
text: vignette demonstrates three distinct ways can use Zresidual evaluate Bayesian models fit via brms: Passing Custom User-Defined Function. Triggering Simulation-Predictive Function. Utilizing Built-Analytic Predictive Function. illustrate methods effectively, establish consistent testing environment. assume environment already contains pre-loaded list 100 datasets (sim_data), true log-odds follow non-linear sine wave. fit two models dataset: misspecified linear model correctly specified non-linear model. MCMC sampling computationally expensive, execute cache 200 model fits disk. subsequent sections, instantly load identical models demonstrate three Zresidual calling methods yield identical, reliable diagnostics.
code: if (!dir.exists(model_dir)) {   dir.create(model_dir, recursive = TRUE) }  n_sim <- 100 n_sample <- 200  sim_data <- vector("list", n_sim)  # Loop through iterations, checking for existing files before simulating/fitting for(i in 1:n_sim) {      data_file <- paste0(model_dir, "/data_sim_", i, ".rds")   wrong_model_file <- paste0(model_dir, "/wrong_sim_", i, ".rds")   correct_model_file <- paste0(model_dir, "/correct_sim_", i, ".rds")      # --- 1. Check and Handle Data ---   if (!force_refit && file.exists(data_file)) {     sim_data[[i]] <- readRDS(data_file)   } else {     # First-time running: Simulate the Bernoulli data     x <- runif(n_sample, 0, 10)     eta <- 2.5 * sin(x)     y <- rbinom(n_sample, 1, plogis(eta))          dat_i <- data.frame(y = y, x = x)     sim_data[[i]] <- dat_i     saveRDS(dat_i, file = data_file)   }      # --- 2. Check and Fit Wrong Model ---   if (force_refit || !file.exists(wrong_model_file)) {     brm(       y ~ x,        family = bernoulli(),        data = sim_data[[i]],       backend = "cmdstanr",       normalize = FALSE,       file = paste0(model_dir, "/wrong_sim_", i),        refresh = 0,       cores = 4     )   }      # --- 3. Check and Fit Correct Model ---   if (force_refit || !file.exists(correct_model_file)) {     brm(       y ~ sin(x),        family = bernoulli(),        data = sim_data[[i]],       backend = "cmdstanr",       normalize = FALSE,       file = paste0(model_dir, "/correct_sim_", i),        refresh = 0,       cores = 4     )   } }

what: Zresidual Using a Custom User-Defined log_pointpred Function
title: Z-residual Diagnostics for brms Logistic Regression
text: Zresidual built highly extensible architecture. specific model family backend natively supported yet, simply want explicit control math, can bypass internal defaults entirely passing custom function log_pointpred argument. , define custom analytical function leverages brms::posterior_epred() manually extract expected probabilities (p) push R’s native distribution functions. evaluate model, explicitly map custom function log_pointpred argument. Zresidual handles rest randomization, prediction extraction, covariate binding automatically.  Replicated p-values fitted models Diagnostic Test p-values Across Randomized Replicates batching custom Zresidual call across 100 simulated datasets, construct sampling distribution. uniform p-values correctly specified model confirm providing user-defined function seamlessly integrates Zresidual statistical pipeline.
code: log_pointpred_brms_bern <- function(fit, data = NULL, ...) {      # 1. Resolve data   data_in <- if (is.null(data)) fit$data else data      # 2. Extract observed response safely   model.data <- model.frame(fit$formula, data = data_in)   model.var  <- names(model.data)      response <- fit$formula$resp   if (is.null(response) || !response %in% model.var) {     response <- all.vars(fit$formula$formula)[1]   }      y_obs <- as.numeric(model.data[[response]])   n <- length(y_obs)      # 3. Extract Posterior Expected Values (probabilities)   p <- brms::posterior_epred(fit, dpar = "mu", newdata = data_in, ...)   mc_used <- nrow(p)      # 4. Calculate Log-PMF and Log-Survival Functions   lpmf <- matrix(NA_real_, mc_used, n)   lsf  <- matrix(NA_real_, mc_used, n)      for (i in seq_len(n)) {     lpmf[, i] <- dbinom(y_obs[i], size = 1, prob = p[, i], log = TRUE)     lsf[, i]  <- pbinom(y_obs[i], size = 1, prob = p[, i],                            lower.tail = FALSE, log.p = TRUE)   }      list(     log_like = lpmf,     log_surv  = lsf,     is_discrete = rep(1L, n)   ) } set.seed(123) ## for reproducibility # Load the first cached dataset and model pair dat <- readRDS(paste0(model_dir, "/data_sim_1.rds"))  fit_wrong <- brm(y ~ x, family = bernoulli(), data = dat, backend = "cmdstanr", file = paste0(model_dir, "/wrong_sim_1")) fit_correct <- brm(y ~ sin(x), family = bernoulli(), data = dat, backend = "cmdstanr", file = paste0(model_dir, "/correct_sim_1"))  # Call Zresidual explicitly using the custom function z_wrong   <- Zresidual(fit_wrong, data = dat, mcmc_summarize = "post", randomized = TRUE, nrep = 10, log_pointpred = log_pointpred_brms_bern) z_correct <- Zresidual(fit_correct, data = dat, mcmc_summarize = "post", randomized = TRUE, nrep = 10, log_pointpred = log_pointpred_brms_bern) i <- 1  par(mfrow = c(2, 3), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1))  qqnorm(z_wrong, irep = i, main = "Wrong: Z-Resid Q-Q") plot(z_wrong, x_axis_var = "x", category = dat$y, irep = i, main = "Wrong: Z-Resid vs X") boxplot(z_wrong, x_axis_var = "lp", irep = i, main = "Wrong: Z-Resid vs LP")  qqnorm(z_correct, irep = i, main = "Correct: Z-Resid Q-Q") plot(z_correct, x_axis_var = "x", category = dat$y, irep = i, main = "Correct: Z-Resid vs X") boxplot(z_correct, x_axis_var = "lp", irep = i, main = "Correct: Z-Resid vs LP") res_table <- data.frame(   "Replicate" = paste("Rep", 1:ncol(z_wrong)),   "SW_W"      = as.numeric(sw.test.zresid(z_wrong)),   "AOV_W"     = as.numeric(aov.test.zresid(z_wrong, X = "lp")),   "BL_W"      = as.numeric(bartlett.test.zresid(z_wrong, X = "lp")),   "SW_C"      = as.numeric(sw.test.zresid(z_correct)),   "AOV_C"     = as.numeric(aov.test.zresid(z_correct, X = "lp")),   "BL_C"      = as.numeric(bartlett.test.zresid(z_correct, X = "lp")) )  res_table %>%   kable(     digits = 4,      col.names = c("Replicate", "SW", "AOV", "Bartlett", "SW", "AOV", "Bartlett"),     caption = "Diagnostic Test p-values Across Randomized Replicates",     align = "lcccccc"   ) %>%   kable_styling(     bootstrap_options = c("striped", "condensed"), full_width = FALSE, position = "center"   ) %>%   add_header_above(c(" " = 1, "Wrong Model (Linear)" = 3, "Correct Model (Non-linear)" = 3)) user_res_file <- paste0(model_dir, "/user_pvalues.rds")  if (!force_recalcz && file.exists(user_res_file)) {   res <- readRDS(user_res_file)   p_c <- res$p_c   p_w <- res$p_w } else {   p_c <- numeric(n_sim)   p_w <- numeric(n_sim)      for(i in 1:n_sim) {     dat_sim <- readRDS(paste0(model_dir, "/data_sim_", i, ".rds"))     fit_w_sim <- brm(y ~ x, family = bernoulli(), data = dat_sim, backend = "cmdstanr", file = paste0(model_dir, "/wrong_sim_", i), refresh = 0)     z_w_sim <- Zresidual(fit_w_sim, data = dat_sim, mcmc_summarize = "post", randomized = TRUE, nrep = 1, log_pointpred = log_pointpred_brms_bern)     p_w[i] <- as.numeric(aov.test.zresid(z_w_sim, X = "lp"))          fit_c_sim <- brm(y ~ sin(x), family = bernoulli(), data = dat_sim, backend = "cmdstanr", file = paste0(model_dir, "/correct_sim_", i), refresh = 0)     z_c_sim <- Zresidual(fit_c_sim, data = dat_sim, mcmc_summarize = "post", randomized = TRUE, nrep = 1, log_pointpred = log_pointpred_brms_bern)     p_c[i] <- as.numeric(aov.test.zresid(z_c_sim, X = "lp"))   }   saveRDS(list(p_c = p_c, p_w = p_w), user_res_file) } par(mfrow = c(1, 2)) hist(p_c, main = "Correct: Model P-values", xlab = "p-value", col = "lightblue", breaks = 10) abline(h = n_sim / 10, col = "red", lty = 2)  hist(p_w, main = "Wrong: Small p-values", xlab = "p-value", col = "salmon", breaks = 10)

Notice drop log_pointpred argument explicitly request "simulation" method. Zresidual automatically handles translation empirical draws normalized residuals. text: vignette demonstrates three distinct ways can use Zresidual evaluate Bayesian models fit via brms: Passing Custom User-Defined Function. Triggering Simulation-Predictive Function. Utilizing Built-Analytic Predictive Function. illustrate methods effectively, first generate consistent testing environment. simulate 100 datasets (true log-rate follows non-linear sine wave) fit two models : misspecified linear model correctly specified non-linear model. MCMC sampling computationally expensive, execute cache 200 model fits disk. subsequent sections, instantly load identical models demonstrate three Zresidual calling methods yield identical, reliable diagnostics.
code: if (!dir.exists(model_dir)) {   dir.create(model_dir, recursive = TRUE) }  n_sim <- 100 n_sample <- 200  sim_data <- vector("list", n_sim)  # Loop through iterations, checking for existing files before simulating/fitting for(i in 1:n_sim) {      data_file <- paste0(model_dir, "/data_sim_", i, ".rds")   wrong_model_file <- paste0(model_dir, "/wrong_sim_", i, ".rds")   correct_model_file <- paste0(model_dir, "/correct_sim_", i, ".rds")      # --- 1. Check and Handle Data ---   if (!force_refit && file.exists(data_file)) {     sim_data[[i]] <- readRDS(data_file)   } else {     x <- runif(n_sample, 0, 10)     y <- rpois(n_sample, exp(1.5 * sin(x)))          dat_i <- data.frame(y = y, x = x)     sim_data[[i]] <- dat_i     saveRDS(dat_i, file = data_file)   }      # --- 2. Check and Fit Wrong Model ---   if (force_refit || !file.exists(wrong_model_file)) {     brm(       y ~ x,        family = poisson(),        data = sim_data[[i]],       backend = "cmdstanr",       normalize = FALSE,       file = paste0(model_dir, "/wrong_sim_", i),        refresh = 0,       cores = 4     )   }      # --- 3. Check and Fit Correct Model ---   if (force_refit || !file.exists(correct_model_file)) {     brm(       y ~ sin(x),        family = poisson(),        data = sim_data[[i]],       backend = "cmdstanr",       normalize = FALSE,       file = paste0(model_dir, "/correct_sim_", i),        refresh = 0,       cores = 4     )   } }

what: Zresidual Using a Custom User-Defined log_pointpred Function
title: Z-residual Diagnostics for brms Poisson Regression
text: Zresidual built highly extensible architecture. specific model family backend natively supported yet, simply want explicit control math, can bypass internal defaults entirely passing custom function log_pointpred argument. , define custom analytical function leverages brms::posterior_epred() manually extract expected rates (\lambda) push R’s native distribution functions. evaluate model, explicitly map custom function log_pointpred argument. Zresidual handles rest randomization, prediction extraction, covariate binding automatically.  Replicated p-values fitted models Diagnostic Test p-values Across Randomized Replicates batching custom Zresidual call across 100 simulated datasets, construct sampling distribution. uniform p-values correctly specified model confirm providing user-defined function seamlessly integrates Zresidual statistical pipeline.
code: log_pointpred_brms_pois <- function(fit, data = NULL, ...) {      # 1. Resolve data   data_in <- if (is.null(data)) fit$data else data      # 2. Extract observed response safely   model.data <- model.frame(fit$formula, data = data_in)   model.var  <- names(model.data)      response <- fit$formula$resp   if (is.null(response) || !response %in% model.var) {     response <- all.vars(fit$formula$formula)[1]   }      y_obs <- as.numeric(model.data[[response]])   n <- length(y_obs)      # 3. Extract Posterior Expected Values (lambda)   lambda <- brms::posterior_epred(fit, dpar = "mu", newdata = data_in, ...)   mc_used <- nrow(lambda)      # 4. Calculate Log-PMF and Log-Survival Functions   lpmf <- matrix(NA_real_, mc_used, n)   lsf  <- matrix(NA_real_, mc_used, n)      for (i in seq_len(n)) {     lpmf[, i] <- dpois(y_obs[i], lambda = lambda[, i], log = TRUE)     lsf[, i]  <- ppois(y_obs[i], lambda = lambda[, i],                            lower.tail = FALSE, log.p = TRUE)   }      list(     log_like = lpmf,     log_surv  = lsf,     is_discrete = rep(1L, n)   ) } set.seed(123) ## for reproducibility # Load the first cached dataset and model pair dat <- readRDS(paste0(model_dir, "/data_sim_1.rds"))  fit_wrong <- brm(y ~ x, family = poisson(), data = dat, backend = "cmdstanr", file = paste0(model_dir, "/wrong_sim_1")) fit_correct <- brm(y ~ sin(x), family = poisson(), data = dat, backend = "cmdstanr", file = paste0(model_dir, "/correct_sim_1"))  # Call Zresidual explicitly using the custom function z_wrong   <- Zresidual(fit_wrong, data = dat, mcmc_summarize = "post", randomized = TRUE, nrep = 10, log_pointpred = log_pointpred_brms_pois) z_correct <- Zresidual(fit_correct, data = dat, mcmc_summarize = "post", randomized = TRUE, nrep = 10, log_pointpred = log_pointpred_brms_pois) i <- 1  par(mfrow = c(2, 3), title: Z-residual Diagnostics for CoxPH Shared Frailty Models
text: vignette explains use Zresidual package calculate Z-residuals based output coxph function survival package R. also demonstrates Z-residuals can used assess overall goodness fit (GOF) identify specific model misspecifications semi-parametric shared frailty models. compannion paper @Wu2025Zresidual

title: Z-residual Diagnostics for CoxPH Shared Frailty Models
text: use Z-residuals diagnose shared frailty models Cox proportional hazards setting baseline function unspecified. group n_i individuals, let y_{ij} possibly right-censored observation \delta_{ij} indicator uncensored. normalized randomized survival probabilities (RSPs) defined : S_{ij}^{R}(y_{ij}, \delta_{ij}, U_{ij}) = \left\{ \begin{array}{rl} S_{ij}(y_{ij}), & \text{} \delta_{ij}=1, \\ U_{ij}\,S_{ij}(y_{ij}), & \text{} \delta_{ij}=0, \end{array} \right. \tag{1} U_{ij} \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1) S_{ij}(\cdot) postulated survival function. RSPs transformed Z-residuals via normal quantile function: r_{ij}^{Z}(y_{ij}, \delta_{ij}, U_{ij})=-\Phi^{-1} (S_{ij}^R(y_{ij}, \delta_{ij}, U_{ij})) \tag{2} true model, Z-residuals normally distributed. transformation allows researchers leverage traditional normal-regression diagnostic tools censored data. Furthermore, normal transformation highlights extreme RSPs may indicate model misspecification otherwise overlooked.

title: Z-residual Diagnostics for CoxPH Shared Frailty Models
text: utilize data 411 acute myeloid leukemia patients recorded M. D. Anderson Cancer Center (1980–1996). dataset focuses patients 60 24 districts. Key variables include survival time, age, sex, white blood cell count (WBC), Townsend deprivation score (TPI). compare two models: wbc model (using raw WBC) lwbc model (using log-transformed WBC). model fitted, calculate Z-residuals models using 50 repetitions. pass traindata = LeukSurv allow internal predictive functions reconstruct baseline hazard. correctly specified shared frailty model, Z-residuals approximately standard normal. animation shows 50 randomization replicates candidate models. Figure 1: Z-residual Q–Q plots overall GOF (animation 50 randomization replicates). Left: WBC model; right: log-WBC model. key Z-residual diagnostic model adequacy homogeneity. sorting observations linear predictor (LP), grouped Z-residuals similar mean variance model adequate. Figure 2: Homogeneity check grouping Z-residuals linear predictor (LP) (animation 50 randomization replicates). diagnose functional-form misspecification, inspect Z-residuals specific covariate. functional form adequate, LOWESS curve remain near 0. Figure 3: Covariate-specific functional-form diagnostic (animation 50 randomization replicates).

logwbc   = log(wbc + 0.001) ) LeukSurv <- LeukSurv[LeukSurv$age < 60, ] fit_wbc <- coxph(Surv(time, cens) ~ age + sex + wbc + tpi +           frailty(district, distribution="gamma"), data = LeukSurv) text: Install Zresidual Loading Required Packages Additional Helper Functions
code: if (!requireNamespace("Zresidual", quietly = TRUE)) {   if (!requireNamespace("remotes", quietly = TRUE)) install.packages("remotes")   remotes::install_github("tiw150/Zresidual", upgrade = "never", dependencies = TRUE) } library(Zresidual)  pkgs <- c(   "survival", "EnvStats", "dplyr", "tibble", "gt", "gifski","MASS" )  missing_pkgs <- pkgs[!vapply(pkgs, requireNamespace, logical(1), quietly = TRUE)] if (length(missing_pkgs)) {   install.packages(missing_pkgs, dependencies = TRUE) }  invisible(lapply(pkgs, function(p) {   suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(p, character.only = TRUE)) })) # Lightweight cumulative martingale comparator used in the original analysis script. lwy_cum_martingale <- function(fit, x, B = 1000, seed = 1) {   r <- residuals(fit, type = "martingale")   ok <- is.finite(r) & is.finite(x)   r  <- r[ok]   x  <- x[ok]    ord   <- order(x)   x_ord <- x[ord]   r_ord <- r[ord]   n     <- length(r_ord)    cum_obs <- cumsum(r_ord) / sqrt(n)   stat_obs <- max(abs(cum_obs))    set.seed(seed)   boot_mat  <- matrix(NA_real_, nrow = B, ncol = n)   boot_stat <- numeric(B)    for (b in seq_len(B)) {     w <- stats::rnorm(n)     cum_b <- cumsum(r_ord * w) / sqrt(n)     boot_mat[b, ] <- cum_b     boot_stat[b]  <- max(abs(cum_b))   }    list(     x = x_ord,     cum = cum_obs,     low = apply(boot_mat, 2, stats::quantile, probs = 0.025, na.rm = TRUE),     high = apply(boot_mat, 2, stats::quantile, probs = 0.975, na.rm = TRUE),     p = mean(boot_stat >= stat_obs)   ) }

title: Z-residuals Diagnostics for Cox Proportional Hazards Models
text: vignette demonstrates use Zresidual package reproduce core workflow Cox proportional hazards paper: compare competing functional forms real-data example, use joint interpretation Z-SW Z-AOV-LP, localize lack fit covariate-specific diagnostics, illustrate contrast functional-form misspecification time-varying effects simulation. practical interpretation : non-significant Z-SW + significant Z-AOV-LP: consistent functional-form misspecification, significant Z-SW + non-significant Z-AOV-LP: consistent non-proportional hazards, significant: broader inadequacy, neither significant: strong evidence immediate revision.

what: A Simulation Study with Non-linear Cox PH Datasets
title: Z-residuals Diagnostics for Cox Proportional Hazards Models
text: Data Generation: setting, data generated proportional-hazards model log(x2) effect, first fit misspecified model using raw x2. Figure 4: comprehensive diagnostic analysis using Z-residuals simulated data. upper row illustrates model diagnostics poorly specified linear-form fit. lower row shows diagnostics correctly specified nonlinear model incorporating log-transformed covariates. perform simulation loop across 500 datasets compare p-values produced Shapiro-Wilk diagnostic test misspecified linear model correctly specified log-nonlinear model. save computation results avoid repeating future renders unless force_rerun enabled.  Distributions Shapiro-Wilk diagnostic test p-values across 500 simulated datasets. Discussion: Correct Model (log-nonlinear), distribution p-values relatively uniform, indicating well-specified functional form yields normally distributed Z-residuals. Wrong Model (linear), vast majority p-values clustered near 0, demonstrating Shapiro-Wilk test reliably detects non-normality caused misspecified functional form.
code: # Functions for Simulating Datasets  rexp2 <- function(n, rate){ if (rate==0) rep(Inf,n) else rexp(n = n, rate = rate)}  ## Genearting Non-linear Cox PH Data  sim_nonlinear_coxph <- function(   sample_size = 500,   lambda = 0.007,   alpha = 3,   mean.censor = 7.5,   beta_a=-2,   beta_b=-2,   beta1 = -0.5,   corr = 0,   t0=pi/2,   seed = 1 ) {   set.seed(seed)   n <- sample_size   Sigma <- matrix(c(2, corr, corr, 2), 2, 2)   X <- MASS::mvrnorm(n = n, mu = c(0, 0), Sigma = Sigma)    x1 <- round(X[, 1], 1)   x2 <- pmax(abs(round(X[, 2], 1)), 0.01)   logx2 <- log(x2)    v <- runif(n)   a<- -log(v)   b<- lambda*exp(x1*beta1+beta_a*log(x2)) * (t0^alpha)   surv_time<-rep(0,n)   group1<-which(a<b)   group2<-which(a>=b)   surv_time[group1]<- (-log(v)[group1]/                          (lambda*exp(x1[group1]*beta1+log(x2)[group1]*beta_a)))^(1 /alpha)      surv_time[group2]<- ((-log(v)[group2]-                           ((t0^alpha)*lambda*                              exp(x1[group2]*beta1+log(x2)[group2]*beta_a))+                           ((t0^alpha)*lambda*                              exp(x1[group2]*beta1+log(x2)[group2]*beta_b)))                        /(lambda*exp(x1[group2]*beta1)                          *exp(log(x2)[group2]*beta_b)))^(1 /alpha)   # Censoring times   c0<- rexp2(n, rate= 1/mean.censor)   data.frame(     x1 = x1,     x2 = x2,     logx2 = logx2,     t = pmin(surv_time, c0),     d = as.integer(surv_time <= c0)   ) }  ## Genearting Time Varying Cox PH Data sim_timevarying_coxph <- function(   sample_size = 500,   lambda = 0.007,   alpha = 3,   mean.censor = 7.5,   beta1 = -2,   beta_a = 1.35,   beta_b = -1.35,   t0 = pi / 2,   corr = 0,   seed = 1 ) {   set.seed(seed)   n <- sample_size   sigma<-rbind(c(2,corr),c(corr,2))   mu<-c(0,0)    covar<-MASS::mvrnorm(n=n, mu=mu, Sigma=sigma)   x1 = round(covar[,1],1)   x2 = round(covar[,2],1)    v <- runif(n)   a<- -log(v)   b<- lambda*exp(x1*beta1+beta_a*x2) * (t0^alpha)   surv_time<-rep(0,n)   group1<-which(a<b)   group2<-which(a>=b)   surv_time[group1]<- (-log(v)[group1]/                          (lambda*exp(x1[group1]*beta1+x2[group1]*beta_a)))^(1 /alpha)      surv_time[group2]<- ((-log(v)[group2]-                           ((t0^alpha)*lambda*                              exp(x1[group2]*beta1+x2[group2]*beta_a))+                           ((t0^alpha)*lambda*                              exp(x1[group2]*beta1+x2[group2]*beta_b)))                        /(lambda*exp(x1[group2]*beta1)                          *exp(x2[group2]*beta_b)))^(1 /alpha)   # Censoring times   c0<- rexp2(n, rate= 1/mean.censor)      data.frame(     x1 = x1,     x2 = x2,     t = pmin(surv_time, c0),     d = as.integer(surv_time <= c0)   ) } dat_nonlinear <- sim_nonlinear_coxph(seed = 101)  fit_linear <- coxph(Surv(t, d) ~ x1 + x2, data = dat_nonlinear) fit_nonlinear <- coxph(Surv(t, d) ~ x1 + log(x2), data = dat_nonlinear)  score_linear <- cox.zph(fit_linear, transform = "identity") score_nonlinear <- cox.zph(fit_nonlinear, transform = "identity")  Z_linear <- Zresidual(fit = fit_linear, data = dat_nonlinear, nrep = 200) Warning in log1p(-exp(diff_ab)): NaNs produced Z_nonlinear <- Zresidual(fit = fit_nonlinear, data = dat_nonlinear, nrep = 200) Warning in log1p(-exp(diff_ab)): NaNs produced zcov_linear    <- Zcov(fit_linear,    data = dat_nonlinear) zcov_nonlinear <- Zcov(fit_nonlinear, data = dat_nonlinear) par(mfrow = c(2, 3), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 3), cex = 1.1)  # Row 1: Misspecified Linear Fit qqnorm(Z_linear, irep = 1, main = "Linear Model Q-Q Z-Residuals") plot(Z_linear, info=zcov_linear, x_axis_var = "lp", irep = 1, add_lowess = TRUE,       main.title = "Linear Model Z-Residuals vs LP") plot(Z_linear, info=zcov_linear, x_axis_var = "x2", irep = 1, add_lowess = TRUE,       main.title = "Linear Model Z-Residuals vs x2")  # Row 2: Correctly Specified Nonlinear Fit qqnorm(Z_nonlinear, irep = 1, main = "Nonlinear Model Q-Q Z-Residuals") plot(Z_nonlinear, info=zcov_nonlinear, x_axis_var = "lp", irep = 1, add_lowess = TRUE,       main.title = "Nonlinear Model Z-Residuals vs LP") plot(Z_nonlinear, info=zcov_nonlinear, x_axis_var = "log(x2)", irep = 1, add_lowess = TRUE,       main.title = "Nonlinear Model Z-Residuals vs log(x2)") sw_linear    <- sw.test.zresid(Z_linear) sw_nonlinear     <- sw.test.zresid(Z_nonlinear) aovlp_linear <- aov.test.zresid(Z_linear, X = "lp",zcov = zcov_linear, k.anova = 10) aovlp_nonlinear  <- aov.test.zresid(Z_nonlinear, X = "lp",zcov = zcov_nonlinear, k.anova = 10) aovx_linear  <- aov.test.zresid(Z_linear, X = "x2",zcov = zcov_linear, k.anova = 10) aovx_nonlinear   <- aov.test.zresid(Z_nonlinear, X = "log(x2)",zcov = zcov_nonlinear, k.anova = 10)  sim_linear_summary <- tibble::tibble(   Model = c("Linear Cox-PH", "Nonlinear Cox-PH"),   `Score global` = c(score_linear$table["GLOBAL", "p"], score_nonlinear$table["GLOBAL", "p"]),   `Score target covariate` = c(score_linear$table["x2", "p"], score_nonlinear$table["log(x2)", "p"]),   `Z-SW` = c(upper_bound_pvalue(sw_linear), upper_bound_pvalue(sw_nonlinear)),   `Z-AOV-LP` = c(upper_bound_pvalue(aovlp_linear), upper_bound_pvalue(aovlp_nonlinear)),   `Z-AOV-target` = c(upper_bound_pvalue(aovx_linear), upper_bound_pvalue(aovx_nonlinear)) )  sim_linear_summary %>%   gt::gt() %>%   gt::fmt_number(columns = -Model, decimals = 3) %>%   gt::tab_header(title = "Summary of Replicated P-values for the Nonlinear Dataset") n_sim <- 500 sim_file <- file.path(resources_path, "sim_sw_pvalues.rds")  if (!force_rerun && file.exists(sim_file)) {   # Load pre-computed results   sim_res <- readRDS(sim_file)   p_c <- sim_res$p_c   p_w <- sim_res$p_w } else {   # Execute the simulation loop   p_c <- numeric(n_sim)   p_w <- numeric(n_sim)      for(i in 1:n_sim) {     # Generate true non-linear data     dat_sim <- sim_nonlinear_coxph(seed = i)          # --- Case 1: Wrong Model (Linear) ---     fit_w_sim <- coxph(Surv(t, d) ~ x1 + x2, data = dat_sim)     z_w_sim   <- Zresidual(fit_w_sim, data = dat_sim, nrep = 1)          p_w[i] <- as.numeric(sw.test.zresid(z_w_sim))          # --- Case 2: Correct Model (Nonlinear) ---     fit_c_sim <- coxph(Surv(t, d) ~ x1 + log(x2), data = dat_sim)     z_c_sim   <- Zresidual(fit_c_sim, data = dat_sim, nrep = 1)          p_c[i] <- as.numeric(sw.test.zresid(z_c_sim))   }      # Save the results for future renders   saveRDS(list(p_c = p_c, p_w = p_w), sim_file) } par(mfrow = c(1, 2)) hist(p_c, main = "Correct: Uniform p-values", xlab = "p-value", col = "lightblue", breaks = 10) abline(h = n_sim / 10, col = "red", lty = 2)  hist(p_w, main = "Wrong: Small p-values", xlab = "p-value", col = "salmon", breaks = 10)

title: Z-residual Diagnostics for glm Logistic Regression
text: Zresidual provides native support models fitted via base R glm() function, need construct custom predictive function. can jump straight simulating dataset. simulate dataset true log-odds follow sine wave. x \[0, 10], average log-odds near zero, naturally keeping prevalence \approx 0.5.
code: n <- 200  x <- runif(n, 0, 10) # True non-linear relationship (sine wave) eta_true <- 2.5 * sin(x)  y <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(eta_true))  dat <- data.frame(y = y, x = x)  cat("Prevalence (proportion of 1s):", mean(dat$y), "\n") Prevalence (proportion of 1s): 0.585

title: Z-residual Diagnostics for glm Logistic Regression
text: compare Wrong Model (assuming linear relationship) Correct Model (specifying sine term).
code: # 1. Wrong Model (Linear) fit_wrong <- glm(y ~ x, family = binomial(), data = dat)  # 2. Correct Model (Non-linear) fit_correct <- glm(y ~ sin(x), family = binomial(), data = dat)  # Calculate Z-residuals using the built-in predictive backend (10 replicates each).  # Covariates (zcov) are computed internally. z_wrong   <- Zresidual(fit_wrong, data = dat, randomized = TRUE, nrep = 10) z_correct <- Zresidual(fit_correct, data = dat, randomized = TRUE, nrep = 10)

title: Z-residual Diagnostics for glm Logistic Regression
text: plots display diagnostics single set Z-residuals.  Note: inspect different realizations randomized residuals, change value (irep index) code chunk . Replicated p-values fitted models Diagnostic Test p-values Across Randomized Replicates
code: # Change this value (1 to 10) to see other replicated plots i <- 1  # Set up 2 rows (Wrong vs Correct) and 3 columns (QQ, Scatter, Box) par(mfrow = c(2, 3), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1))  # --- ROW 1: WRONG MODEL (Linear) --- # 1. Q-Q Plot qqnorm(z_wrong, irep = i, main = "Wrong: Z-Resid Q-Q") qqline(as.vector(z_wrong[, i]), col = "red")  # 2. Scatter Plot (Note the wave pattern in the residuals) plot(z_wrong, x_axis_var = "lp", category = dat$y,       irep = i, add_lowess = TRUE, main = "Wrong: Z-Resid vs LP")  # 3. Boxplot boxplot(z_wrong, x_axis_var = "x", num.bin = 10,          irep = i, main = "Wrong: Z-Resid vs X")  # --- ROW 2: CORRECT MODEL (Non-linear) --- # 1. Q-Q Plot qqnorm(z_correct, irep = i, main = "Correct: Z-Resid Q-Q") qqline(as.vector(z_correct[, i]), col = "blue")  # 2. Scatter Plot (Residuals look like random noise) plot(z_correct, x_axis_var = "lp", category = dat$y,       irep = i, add_lowess = TRUE, main = "Correct: Z-Resid vs LP")  # 3. Boxplot boxplot(z_correct, x_axis_var = "x", num.bin = 10,          irep = i, main = "Correct: Z-Resid vs X") library(knitr) library(kableExtra)  # 1. Create the data frame  res_table <- data.frame(   "Replicate" = paste("Rep", 1:ncol(z_wrong)),   "SW_W"      = as.numeric(sw.test.zresid(z_wrong)),   "AOV_W"     = as.numeric(aov.test.zresid(z_wrong, X = "x")),   "BL_W"      = as.numeric(bartlett.test.zresid(z_wrong, X = "x")),   "SW_C"      = as.numeric(sw.test.zresid(z_correct)),   "AOV_C"     = as.numeric(aov.test.zresid(z_correct, X = "x")),   "BL_C"      = as.numeric(bartlett.test.zresid(z_correct, X = "x")) )  # 2. Render with minimalistic styling and spanning headers res_table %>%   kable(     digits = 4,      # Base column names (the bottom row of the header)     col.names = c("Replicate", "SW", "AOV", "Bartlett", "SW", "AOV", "Bartlett"),     caption = "Diagnostic Test p-values Across Randomized Replicates",     align = "lcccccc"   ) %>%   kable_styling(     bootstrap_options = c("striped", "condensed"),      full_width = FALSE,     position = "center"   ) %>%   # Add the spanning header (the top row)   add_header_above(c(" " = 1, "Wrong Model (Linear)" = 3, "Correct Model (Non-linear)" = 3))

what: 4. Sampling Distribution of AOV Nonlinearity Tests
title: Z-residual Diagnostics for glm Logistic Regression
text: run simulation loop verify built-implementation correctly handles binomial data produces appropriate p-values.  Discussion: Correct Model, distribution p-values uniform, indicating well-specified model. Wrong Model, p-values small (clustered near 0), indicating ANOVA test successfully detected non-linear trend.
code: n_sim <- 100 sim_file <- paste0(file_prefix, "simulation_results.rds")  if (!force_rerun && file.exists(sim_file)) {   sim_res <- readRDS(sim_file)   p_c <- sim_res$p_c   p_w <- sim_res$p_w } else {   p_c <- numeric(n_sim)   p_w <- numeric(n_sim)   n_sample <- 200     for(i in 1:n_sim) {     x_sim <- runif(n_sample, 0, 10)          # Generate true non-linear data     eta_sim <- 2.5 * sin(x_sim)     y_sim   <- rbinom(n_sample, 1, plogis(eta_sim))     dat_sim <- data.frame(y = y_sim, x = x_sim)          # --- Case 1: Wrong Model (Linear) ---     fit_w <- glm(y ~ x, family = binomial(), data = dat_sim)     z_w   <- Zresidual(fit_w, data = dat_sim, randomized = TRUE, nrep = 1)     p_w[i] <- as.numeric(aov.test.zresid(z_w, X = "x"))          # --- Case 2: Correct Model (Sine) ---     fit_c <- glm(y ~ sin(x), family = binomial(), data = dat_sim)     z_c   <- Zresidual(fit_c, data = dat_sim, randomized = TRUE, nrep = 1)     p_c[i] <- as.numeric(aov.test.zresid(z_c, X = "x"))   }      saveRDS(list(p_c = p_c, p_w = p_w), sim_file) } par(mfrow = c(1, 2)) hist(p_c, main = "Correct: Uniform p-values", xlab = "p-value", col = "lightblue", breaks = 20) abline(h = n_sim / 20, col = "red", lty = 2)  hist(p_w, main = "Wrong: Small p-values", xlab = "p-value", col = "salmon", breaks = 20)

title: Z-residual Diagnostics for glm Poisson Regression
text: rigorously evaluate methodology ensure direct comparability brms demonstration, utilize exact 100 independent count datasets true log-rate follows non-linear sine wave continuous variable x. standard frequentist glm() fits computationally instantaneous, need cache fitted model objects hard drive. simply load cached datasets resources/brms_poission_models/ fit models fly.
code: if (!dir.exists(model_dir)) {   dir.create(model_dir, recursive = TRUE) } set.seed(2026) ## for reproducing the same datasets n_sim <- 100 n_sample <- 200  sim_data <- vector("list", n_sim)  # Loop through iterations, checking for existing data files for(i in 1:n_sim) {   data_file <- paste0(model_dir, "/data_sim_", i, ".rds")      if (!force_refit && file.exists(data_file)) {     # If data exists from the brms run, read it     sim_data[[i]] <- readRDS(data_file)   } else {     # Otherwise, simulate and save it     x <- runif(n_sample, 0, 10)     y <- rpois(n_sample, exp(1.5 * sin(x)))          dat_i <- data.frame(y = y, x = x)     sim_data[[i]] <- dat_i     saveRDS(dat_i, file = data_file)   } }

what: Z-residual Using a Custom User-Defined log_pointpred Function
title: Z-residual Diagnostics for glm Poisson Regression
text: Since Zresidual highly extensible, can easily handle standard frequentist models like glm. frequentist model can viewed “Bayesian” model posterior consisting exactly 1 draw (point estimate). , define custom analytic function log_pointpred_glm_poisson extracts predicted rates (\lambda) using standard predict() function structures 1 \times N matrix format expected Zresidual. retrieve first cached dataset evaluate glm fits using custom function.  Replicated p-values fitted models Diagnostic Test p-values Across Randomized Replicates utilize identical cached datasets verify custom glm implementation correctly detects misspecifications across 100 simulations.  Discussion: uniform distribution p-values correctly specified glm model confirms Zresidual perfectly handles standard frequentist output provided 1 \times N matrix via custom predictive function. Conversely, strongly skewed distribution linear specification demonstrates test reliably catches structural misspecifications, mirroring results saw Bayesian brms framework.
code: log_pointpred_glm_poisson <- function(fit, data = NULL, ...) {      # 1. Resolve data   data_in <- if (is.null(data)) model.frame(fit) else data      # 2. Extract observed response   # For glm objects, the response variable name can be extracted from the formula   resp_var <- all.vars(formula(fit))[1]   y_obs <- as.numeric(data_in[[resp_var]])   n <- length(y_obs)      # 3. Extract Point Estimate Expected Values (lambda)   # type = "response" gives the predicted rate (lambda) on the outcome scale   lambda <- predict(fit, newdata = data_in, type = "response")      # 4. title: Z-residual: A Unified Gaussian-like Residual for Checking Bayesian and Frequentist Models with Continuous, Discrete, and Censored Response
text: Zresidual package implements diagnostic residuals based predictive distribution observation. utilizing full probabilistic information model, package generates residuals approximately normally distributed, enabling standard graphical numerical diagnostics identical used Gaussian OLS. foundation approach Randomized Survival Probability (RSP). given observation y_i, RSP represents value predictive survival function y_i, incorporating randomization term ensure continuity discrete outcomes. defined Equation 1: \text{RSP}_i(y_i | \boldsymbol{\theta}) = S_i(y_i | \boldsymbol{\theta}) + U_i \, p_i(y_i | \boldsymbol{\theta}) \tag{1} : S_i(y_i | \boldsymbol{\theta}) = P(Y_i > y_i | \boldsymbol{\theta}) survival function (probability new observation strictly exceed observed value). p_i(y_i | \boldsymbol{\theta}) = P(Y_i = y_i | \boldsymbol{\theta}) probability mass function (PMF). continuous outcomes, p_i(y_i) = 0, term drops entirely. U_i \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1) random uniform variable. role continuously “smear” discrete probability mass, ensuring resulting RSP suffer discrete steps. correctly specified model, RSP follows uniform U(0,1) distribution. Z-residual derived transforming RSP via inverse standard normal cumulative distribution function (\Phi^{-1}), shown Equation 2: z_i(y_i | \boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\Phi^{-1}[\text{RSP}_i(y_i | \boldsymbol{\theta})] \tag{2} correct model specification, residuals follow standard normal N(0,1) distribution. mapping allows researchers assess quality predictive distribution—including mean, variance, shape—using familiar, highly sensitive Gaussian diagnostics. survival analysis, framework naturally extends carefully defining predictive distribution observed outcome. Let T_i true underlying event time continuous survival function S_i^*(t | \boldsymbol{\theta}) = P(T_i > t | \boldsymbol{\theta}). Let C_i censoring time, assumed independent T_i. observed outcome Y_i = \min(T_i, C_i), record event indicator \delta_i = (T_i \le C_i). Thus, observed data point y_i, dual nature depending \delta_i: event observed (\delta_i = 1): Y_i behaves continuous variable. probability surviving strictly past y_i S_i(y_i | \boldsymbol{\theta}) = S_i^*(y_i | \boldsymbol{\theta}). outcome continuous point, probability mass zero: p_i(y_i | \boldsymbol{\theta}) = 0. event right-censored (\delta_i = 0): know T_i > C_i = y_i. perspective observed variable Y_i, point y_i acts accumulation probability mass representing possible future event times. Therefore, probability strictly surviving past censored point zero: S_i(y_i | \boldsymbol{\theta}) = 0. probability mass point exactly probability true event happens censoring time: p_i(y_i | \boldsymbol{\theta}) = S_i^*(y_i | \boldsymbol{\theta}). Formally, predictive survival probability mass functions observed outcome Y_i defined : S_i(y_i | \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \begin{cases} S_i^*(y_i | \boldsymbol{\theta}), & \text{} \delta_i = 1 \\ 0, & \text{} \delta_i = 0 \end{cases} \tag{3} p_i(y_i | \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \begin{cases} 0, & \text{} \delta_i = 1 \\ S_i^*(y_i | \boldsymbol{\theta}), & \text{} \delta_i = 0 \end{cases} \tag{4} substituting context-specific definitions S_i(y_i | \boldsymbol{\theta}) p_i(y_i | \boldsymbol{\theta}) back general RSP equation Equation 1, obtain unified, piecewise formula censored data Equation 5: \text{RSP}(y_i | \delta_i,\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \left\{ \begin{array}{rl} S_i^*(y_i | \boldsymbol{\theta}), & \text{} \delta_i=1 \\ U_i \, S_i^*(y_i | \boldsymbol{\theta}), & \text{} \delta_i=0 \end{array} \right. \tag{5} Just standard regression, RSPs transformed Z-residuals via normal quantile function, defined Equation 6: z_i(y_i, \delta_i, U_i) = -\Phi^{-1} (\text{RSP}(y_i| \delta_i, \boldsymbol{\theta})) \tag{6} Normal transformation highlights extreme RSPs indicate model misspecification (proportional hazards violations) often masked standard Martingale Cox-Snell residuals.

text: animation Figure 2 demonstrates behavior Z-residuals model misspecified. , data y_i simulated True Model (Negative Binomial \mu=2), residuals calculated based Postulated Model (Negative Binomial \mu=5). can see observed data (solid bars) fall systematically left expected distribution (dashed bars). observed values smaller expected, calculated RSP values consistently high (upper tail area large), resulting z_i values drift toward negative side standard normal distribution. Figure 2: Animation Z-residual wrong model. Data generated True Model \mu=2, residuals calculated using Postulated Model \mu=5. observed values y_i likely smaller expected, resulting RSP values (blue bars) large assumed distribution. Consequently, Z-residuals drift systematically left (negative bias), signaling model overestimates mean.

title: Z-residual: Computing and Diagnosing Gaussian-like Residuals
text: Zresidual package implements diagnostic residuals based predictive distribution observation. utilizing full probabilistic information model, package generates residuals approximately normally distributed, enabling standard diagnostics Pearson’s residuals OLS. foundation approach Randomized Survival Probability (RSP). given observation y_i, RSP represents value predictive cumulative distribution function (CDF) y_i, randomization term ensure continuity discrete outcomes. defined : RSP_i(y_i \mid \theta) = S_i(y_i \mid \theta) + U_i \, p_i(y_i \mid \theta) S_i p_i represent survival function probability mass function, respectively, derived predictive distribution y_i given covariates \mathbf{x}_i parameters \theta. U_i \sim \text{Unif}(0,1) random uniform variable used handle discrete outcomes. correctly specified model observed data y_i arises assumed predictive distribution, RSP follows \text{Unif}(0,1) distribution. Z-residual derived transforming RSP via inverse standard normal cumulative distribution function: z_i^{RSP}(y_i \mid \theta) = -\Phi^{-1}[RSP_i(y_i \mid \theta)] correct model specification, residuals follow standard normal N(0,1) distribution. mapping allows researchers assess quality predictive distribution—including mean, variance, shape—using standard diagnostics Gaussian OLS. practical applications, parameter vector \theta must estimated. package supports two primary paradigms constructing residuals:

title: Z-residual: Computing and Diagnosing Gaussian-like Residuals
text: frequentist models (e.g., survival, glmmTMB), package computes residuals plugging estimated parameters \hat{\theta}. Furthermore, coxph models, package provides cross-validatory Z-residuals allow rigorous validation survival models within frequentist framework.

title: Z-residual: Computing and Diagnosing Gaussian-like Residuals
text: Bayesian models (e.g., brms), package accounts parameter uncertainty integrating posterior distribution: Posterior Z-residuals: obtained averaging RSPs full-data posterior f(\theta \mid y). Although approach involves “double use data” since observations estimation residual calculation, z-residuals asymptotically distributed N(0,1) test p-values asymptotically Uniform (0,1) approach “summarize first test” used. Importance-Sampling Cross-Validatory (ISCV) Z-residuals: provide robust diagnostic, package implements ISCV Z-residuals. method approximates leave-one-(LOO) RSPs using importance sampling based full-data posterior. deleted residuals OLS, ISCV Z-residuals variance slightly greater 1. resulting posterior ISCV Z-residuals approximately standard normal, enabling full range graphical numerical diagnostics Bayesian models.

title: Z-residual: Computing and Diagnosing Gaussian-like Residuals
text: Zresidual package provides built-support objects generated several prominent R modeling packages. current implementation includes: Custom Models: Support user-defined framework providing log_cdf log_pmf arguments generic Zresidual method. survival: Support Cox Proportional Hazards models (coxph) parametric survival models (survreg). brms: Support wide array Bayesian regression models via brmsfit interface. stats: Support standard Generalized Linear Models (glm).

title: Extract aligned model metadata for Z-residual diagnostics — Zcov
text: Zcov(brmsfit): Method brmsfit objects. Currently, top-level type argument implemented hurdle zero-inflated models. models, setting type = "zero" isolates binary zero-inflation/hurdle process, type = "count" isolates truncated count process, type = "hurdle" evaluates entire model. standard families (e.g., standard logistic, Poisson, Gaussian), type argument unnecessary ignored warning. standard models, function simply returns standard linear predictors, covariates, response. Zcov(coxph): Method coxph objects survival package. Extracts Surv response, linear predictors, censoring metadata. Zcov(survreg): Method survreg objects survival package. Extracts Surv response, linear predictors, distribution scale, censoring metadata. Zcov(glm): Method glm objects. Standard generalized linear models single-process models. Therefore, type argument unnecessary ignored warning provided. function returns standard linear predictors link scale, covariates, response.

title: Extract aligned model metadata for Z-residual diagnostics — Zcov
code: if (requireNamespace("survival", quietly = TRUE)) {   set.seed(1)   n <- 20   x <- rnorm(n)   t_event <- rexp(n, rate = exp(0.2 * x))   t_cens  <- rexp(n, rate = 0.5)   status  <- as.integer(t_event <= t_cens)   time    <- pmin(t_event, t_cens)   dat <- data.frame(time = time, status = status, x = x)    fit <- survival::coxph(survival::Surv(time, status) ~ x, data = dat)   info <- Zcov(fit, data = dat)   names(info) } #>  [1] "type"          "family"        "response_name" "response"      #>  [5] "covariates"    "linear_pred"   "obs_id"        "y_type"        #>  [9] "y_type_kind"   "y_type_levels" "extra"

text: input x typically obtained residual functions package (e.g., residual.coxph(), residual.coxph.frailty(), residual.survreg()) residual.type = "Cox-Snell". title: Cox–Snell residual plot for survival models — plot.cs.residual
code: if (requireNamespace("survival", quietly = TRUE)) {   set.seed(1)   n <- 30   x <- rnorm(n)   t_event <- rexp(n, rate = exp(0.2 * x))   t_cens  <- rexp(n, rate = 0.5)   status  <- as.integer(t_event <= t_cens)   time    <- pmin(t_event, t_cens)   dat <- data.frame(time = time, status = status, x = x)   fit <- survival::coxph(survival::Surv(time, status) ~ x, data = dat)   r <- surv_residuals(fit, data = dat, residual.type = "Cox-Snell")   plot(r) }

title: Compute residual diagnostics for supported survival models — surv_residuals
code: if (requireNamespace("survival", quietly = TRUE)) {   set.seed(1)   n <- 30   x <- rnorm(n)   t_event <- rexp(n, rate = exp(0.3 * x))   t_cens  <- rexp(n, rate = 0.5)   status  <- as.integer(t_event <= t_cens)   time    <- pmin(t_event, t_cens)   dat <- data.frame(time = time, status = status, x = x)    fit <- survival::coxph(survival::Surv(time, status) ~ x, data = dat)   r <- surv_residuals(fit, data = dat, residual.type = "martingale")   head(as.vector(r)) } #> [1]  0.9017249 -0.9004452  0.7137561  0.4771257  0.3631152  0.8328093
